title: Test-Driven Thinking
intro: Before you even write test code, you can start thinking like a tester. Test-driven thinking means asking 'how would I verify this works?' before you build something. Let's practice this mindset.

teach.title: Thinking Like a Tester = Asking 'What Could Go Wrong?'
teach.body: When you cross a street, you automatically check for cars. You don't just walk out blindly. **Test-driven thinking** is the same instinct applied to code. Before writing a function, you think: what inputs could break this? What should happen with weird data? What if the user does something unexpected?

teach.title: Test-Driven Thinking = Writing the Exam Before the Textbook
teach.body: Imagine a teacher who writes the final exam FIRST, then designs the course to make sure students can pass it. That's **test-driven thinking**. You define what success looks like (the test) before you build the thing (the code). This way, you never lose sight of what 'done' means, and you know the moment you've achieved it.

teach.title: The Test-Driven Mindset
teach.body:
Before writing any code, ask yourself:

1. **What should this function do?** -- Define the expected behavior
2. **What are the normal inputs?** -- The typical use case
3. **What are the edge cases?** -- Empty strings, zero, null, negative numbers
4. **What should happen when things go wrong?** -- Error messages, defaults, fallbacks

If you can answer these questions, you can write tests.

teach.title: Thinking Through a Function
teach.annotation: Notice how thinking about tests BEFORE writing code helps you design a better function. You discover edge cases early.

teach.body: When you tell AI what to build, you're already thinking about requirements. Turn those requirements into tests and you've just built a safety net that protects your app -- and your income -- every time the AI writes new code.
Test-driven thinking means defining expected behavior before writing code. Ask: what should this do, what are the edge cases, and what should happen when things go wrong?

steps.instruction: You need to build a password validator. Before writing any code, let's think about what to test. First, run the test suite to see the current state.
steps.output:
PASS  tests/utils.test.js

Test Suites: 1 passed, 1 total
Tests:       0 total
Time:        0.5s

No tests found. Your test file is empty -- time to add some!
steps.hint: Run the test suite with the standard npm command for testing.

steps.instruction: Now let's think test-first. Open the test file to write our expectations BEFORE writing the actual function. View the test file.
steps.hint: Use 'cat' to view the contents of the test file.

steps.instruction: The test file has prompts to think about. Now run the tests again -- they should fail since we haven't written the function yet. This is the TDD way: write failing tests first.
steps.output:
FAIL  tests/utils.test.js
  validatePassword
    ✗ rejects passwords shorter than 8 characters (2ms)
    ✗ accepts passwords 8 characters or longer (1ms)
    ✗ rejects passwords without a number (1ms)
    ✗ handles empty string input (1ms)

Tests: 4 failed, 4 total
Time: 0.8s

All tests failing -- exactly what we want! Now write the function to make them pass.
steps.hint: Run npm test again to see the failing tests.

steps.instruction: The tests are failing as expected. This is the Red-Green-Refactor cycle: Red (tests fail), Green (write code to pass), Refactor (clean up). Now let's check what the actual validator function looks like.
steps.hint: Use 'cat' to view the source code file.

exercise.completionMessage: You've practiced test-driven thinking! The key insight is: thinking about tests before code forces you to clearly define what 'working' means. You discovered edge cases (empty string, no numbers) before writing a single line of implementation code.
